title: VPC & Networking Foundation
description:
## Summary
Provision the foundational AWS VPC and networking infrastructure that all other AWS resources depend on.

## Region & CIDR Blocks
- **Region**: us-east-2
- **Staging VPC**: 10.0.0.0/16
- **Prod VPC**: 10.1.0.0/16

Non-overlapping CIDRs allow future VPC peering if needed.

## Scope
Terraform modules for:
- VPC with public/private/database subnets across 2-3 AZs
- fck-nat instance for private subnet egress (cost-optimized)
- Internet gateway for public subnets
- Route tables (public, private, database)
- Security groups (baseline, can be extended by dependent epics)
- S3 Gateway Endpoint (free) for ECR layers and logs

## Environment Strategy
Two AWS environments, both in the same account (can split later):

| Environment | Purpose | VPC CIDR |
|-------------|---------|----------|
| Staging | CI/CD, E2E tests, integration tests | 10.0.0.0/16 |
| Prod | Real users, isolated | 10.1.0.0/16 |

Local development uses Docker Compose (Postgres, Redis, mock auth) - no AWS resources.

The VPC module is instantiated twice with different tfvars (staging.tfvars, prod.tfvars).

## Subnet Tiers
| Tier | Purpose | Internet Access |
|------|---------|-----------------|
| Public | ALB, NAT | Direct (IGW) |
| Private | EKS pods, apps | Outbound only (NAT) |
| Database | RDS, Redis | None (isolated) |

## Key Decisions
- CIDR block sizing: /16 VPC with /20 subnets (4k IPs per subnet)
- AZ count: 2 for cost efficiency (can expand to 3 later)
- NAT: fck-nat on t4g.nano (~$3/mo) instead of NAT Gateway (~$32/mo)
- VPC Endpoints: S3 gateway only (free). Skip interface endpoints (~$7/mo each) - use fck-nat instead.
- DB access: Use kubectl exec into pods, no bastion needed
- Environments: Staging + Prod only. Local dev via Docker Compose.

## Deferred (add later if needed)
- **CloudFront**: Not needed initially. Easy to add in front of ALB later.
- **VPC Flow Logs**: Enable when debugging network issues. ~$0.50/GB to CloudWatch.
- **Route53**: DNS managed externally. ALB DNS name output is sufficient for CNAME setup.
- **Bastion/VPN**: Use kubectl exec or debug pods for DB access instead.
- **Tagging strategy**: Basic environment tags for now. Per-customer resource tagging will be important later for cost allocation.
- **Multi-account**: Prod can move to separate AWS account later if needed (~2 hours of work).

## Deliverables
- Terraform module: `infrastructure/terraform/modules/vpc/`
- Environment configs: staging.tfvars, prod.tfvars
- Outputs: VPC ID, subnet IDs (public, private, database), security group IDs

## References
- See `/docs/MIGRATION.md` Phase 1: Infrastructure Foundation
- See `/docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` for overall AWS topology and environment strategy
- fck-nat: https://fck-nat.dev/

title: Create VPC Terraform module structure
description:
Set up base Terraform module at infrastructure/terraform/modules/vpc/ with main.tf, variables.tf, outputs.tf, versions.tf. Define input variables for environment, region, CIDR block, AZ count.

**Done when:**
- [ ] Directory structure exists: `infrastructure/terraform/modules/vpc/`
- [ ] Files created: main.tf, variables.tf, outputs.tf, versions.tf
- [ ] Variables defined: environment, region, vpc_cidr, az_count
- [ ] `terraform validate` passes

title: Implement VPC with public/private/database subnets
description:
Create VPC with /16 CIDR block. Implement three subnet tiers across 2 AZs (expandable to 3):

- **Public subnets** (/20, ~4k IPs): ALB, NAT instance
- **Private subnets** (/20, ~4k IPs): EKS Fargate pods, application workloads
- **Database subnets** (/20, ~4k IPs): RDS, ElastiCache - fully isolated, no NAT route

Database subnets have no route to internet (inbound or outbound). Only accessible from private subnets via security groups.

Use data source for available AZs in us-east-2.

**CIDR blocks** (from parent epic):
- Staging: 10.0.0.0/16
- Prod: 10.1.0.0/16

**Done when:**
- [ ] VPC created with /16 CIDR
- [ ] `enable_dns_hostnames = true` and `enable_dns_support = true` configured
- [ ] 2 public subnets (one per AZ)
- [ ] 2 private subnets (one per AZ)
- [ ] 2 database subnets (one per AZ)
- [ ] `terraform plan` shows expected resources
- [ ] Subnets tagged with tier (public/private/database)

title: Implement Internet Gateway and NAT Gateways
description:
Create Internet Gateway for public subnets. Deploy fck-nat instance (t4g.nano) for NAT instead of NAT Gateway to minimize costs (~$3/mo vs ~$32/mo). Single NAT instance initially - can upgrade to NAT Gateway later if needed. Use Elastic IP for the NAT instance. Reference: https://fck-nat.dev/

**fck-nat configuration:**
- Instance type: t4g.nano (ARM, ~$3/mo)
- AMI: Use fck-nat community AMI for us-east-2
- Placement: First public subnet (us-east-2a)
- Elastic IP: Associate with ENI

**Critical**: The ENI must have `source_dest_check = false` to allow traffic to flow through the instance (hairpinning). Without this, NAT will not work.

**Done when:**
- [ ] Internet Gateway created and attached to VPC
- [ ] Elastic IP allocated for NAT
- [ ] fck-nat instance launched in public subnet with EIP
- [ ] ENI has `source_dest_check = false`
- [ ] NAT instance can reach internet (test: curl from instance)

title: Implement route tables for public and private subnets
description:
Create route tables for each subnet tier:

- **Public route table**: Default route (0.0.0.0/0) to Internet Gateway. Associate with public subnets.
- **Private route table**: Default route to fck-nat instance. Associate with private subnets.
- **Database route table**: No default route (fully isolated). Associate with database subnets.

Database tier has no outbound internet path - RDS/Redis can only be reached from within the VPC.

**Done when:**
- [ ] Public route table with 0.0.0.0/0 → IGW
- [ ] Private route table with 0.0.0.0/0 → fck-nat ENI
- [ ] Database route table with local routes only
- [ ] Each subnet associated with correct route table

title: Implement baseline security groups (Fargate model)
description:
Create baseline security groups for Fargate-based EKS (no EC2 nodes):

- **ALB SG**: Inbound 80/443 from 0.0.0.0/0, outbound to EKS Pod SG
- **EKS Pod SG**: Inbound from ALB SG on app ports, outbound to RDS SG, Redis SG, and 0.0.0.0/0 (internet via NAT). Attached to Fargate pod ENIs via EKS SecurityGroupPolicy.
- **RDS SG**: Inbound 5432 from EKS Pod SG only
- **Redis SG**: Inbound 6379 from EKS Pod SG only
- **NAT SG**: Inbound all traffic from VPC CIDR, outbound all traffic to 0.0.0.0/0. Per fck-nat docs, traffic flows THROUGH the instance (hairpinning), not TO it.

Note: Fargate pods get individual ENIs with VPC IPs - no "node" security group needed. The EKS Pod SG is applied to pod ENIs, not EC2 instances.

**Important**: The fck-nat ENI must have `source_dest_check = false` to allow pass-through traffic. This is configured in PLAT-03e.3 (NAT instance task).

**Done when:**
- [ ] All 5 security groups created
- [ ] NAT SG allows all traffic from VPC CIDR inbound
- [ ] Rules reference other SGs by ID (not CIDR) where applicable
- [ ] Security group IDs exported as outputs

title: Implement S3 Gateway Endpoint
description:
Create S3 Gateway Endpoint (free) for ECR image layers, logs, and Terraform state. Skip interface endpoints (ECR API, CloudWatch, STS, Secrets Manager) - let this traffic go through fck-nat instead. Interface endpoints cost ~$7/mo/AZ each which defeats the cost savings from fck-nat.

Future consideration: Add interface endpoints if fck-nat becomes a bottleneck or if security requirements mandate traffic stay within VPC.

**Done when:**
- [ ] S3 Gateway Endpoint created
- [ ] Endpoint associated with private and database route tables
- [ ] S3 traffic from private subnets does not go through NAT (verify in route table)

title: Create environment configs (staging, prod)
description:
Create tfvars files for staging and prod environments. Both use single fck-nat instance initially.

**Environment configs:**
- staging.tfvars: us-east-2, 10.0.0.0/16, 2 AZs, single NAT
- prod.tfvars: us-east-2, 10.1.0.0/16, 2 AZs, single NAT

Local development does not use AWS - uses Docker Compose instead.

**Terraform organization:**
```
infrastructure/terraform/
├── modules/vpc/           # Reusable VPC module
├── environments/
│   ├── staging/
│   │   ├── main.tf        # Instantiates modules
│   │   ├── backend.tf     # S3 state (staging prefix)
│   │   └── terraform.tfvars
│   └── prod/
│       ├── main.tf
│       ├── backend.tf     # S3 state (prod prefix)
│       └── terraform.tfvars
```

**Done when:**
- [ ] tfvars files created for staging, prod with correct CIDR blocks
- [ ] `terraform plan` succeeds for both environments
- [ ] State stored in separate S3 prefixes (staging/, prod/)
- [ ] VPC module outputs exported: vpc_id, vpc_cidr, public_subnet_ids, private_subnet_ids, database_subnet_ids, alb_sg_id, eks_pod_sg_id, rds_sg_id, redis_sg_id, nat_sg_id

title: Bootstrap Terraform state backend
description:
Create S3 bucket and DynamoDB table for Terraform remote state before any other infrastructure.

**Resources:**
- S3 bucket: `eval-terraform-state` with versioning enabled
- DynamoDB table: `terraform-locks` with PAY_PER_REQUEST billing, LockID (string) as hash key

**Bootstrap process:**
1. Create `infrastructure/terraform/bootstrap/` with state backend resources
2. Apply locally (state stored in git initially, one-time only)
3. Configure backend in `infrastructure/terraform/environments/` to use remote state
4. All subsequent terraform runs use S3 + DynamoDB

**S3 bucket settings:**
- Versioning: enabled (recover from bad applies)
- Encryption: AES-256 (SSE-S3)
- Public access: blocked
- Lifecycle: keep all versions (state history)

**Done when:**
- [ ] S3 bucket `eval-terraform-state` exists with versioning enabled
- [ ] DynamoDB table `terraform-locks` exists with LockID key
- [ ] `terraform init` in environments/ configures remote backend successfully
- [ ] `terraform plan` acquires and releases lock
close_reason: Implemented and reviewed - Bootstrap module complete with S3 (AES256 encryption, versioning, public access blocked) and DynamoDB (terraform-locks, PAY_PER_REQUEST). terraform validate passes. Actual apply requires AWS credentials.

title: Identity Platform SAML Integration
description:
## Summary
Configure Identity Platform for SAML-based authentication with enterprise identity providers (university SSO systems).

## Scope
- SAML Identity Provider configuration in Identity Platform
- Attribute mapping from SAML assertions
- Testing with a mock SAML IdP

## SAML Configuration (Manual in GCP Console)
1. Go to: https://console.cloud.google.com/customer-identity/providers
2. Click "Add a Provider" > "SAML"
3. Configure:
   - Provider name
   - IdP Entity ID
   - SSO URL
   - X.509 Certificate
4. Note the SP Entity ID and ACS URL for your IdP

## SAML Attribute Mapping Strategy
Map standard attributes:
- `email` → email
- `given_name` → first name
- `family_name` → last name

**IdP-specific attributes** (eduPersonAffiliation, eppn, department, etc.) are captured by the Go API on first login and stored in the application database per-tenant.

## Flows to Support
1. SP-initiated SSO (user clicks "Login with SSO" → redirect to IdP)
2. IdP-initiated SSO (user starts at IdP, lands at app)

## Deliverables
- SAML provider configured in Identity Platform
- Documentation on claim passthrough
- Mock SAML IdP setup for testing (e.g., samlidp, Keycloak)
- Integration test scripts

## Dependencies
- Requires: Identity Platform base setup (PLAT-dar)

## References
- GCP Identity Platform SAML docs
- See `/docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` for auth flow overview

title: Database Schema & Row-Level Security
description:
## Summary
Design and implement the PostgreSQL database schema with Row-Level Security (RLS) policies for multi-tenant isolation.

## Scope
- Complete database schema (tables, indexes, constraints)
- RLS policies using PostgreSQL session variables
- Database migrations tooling (golang-migrate or similar)
- Seed data for development/testing

## Core Tables (based on existing app)
- `namespaces` - multi-tenant organizations
- `users` - user accounts (linked to Cognito)
- `classes` - courses
- `sections` - class sections
- `problems` - assignment problems
- `sessions` - live coding sessions
- `invitations` - user invitations

## RLS Implementation
```sql
-- Example RLS policy pattern
ALTER TABLE classes ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY;
CREATE POLICY tenant_isolation ON classes
  USING (namespace_id = current_setting('app.namespace_id', true)::uuid);
```

Session variables set by middleware:
- `app.user_id` - current user UUID
- `app.namespace_id` - current tenant UUID
- `app.role` - user role for permission checks

## Deliverables
- SQL migration files: `go-backend/migrations/`
- Schema documentation
- RLS policy test suite
- Development seed data script

## Dependencies
- Requires: RDS PostgreSQL Database (PLAT-etu)

## References
- See `/docs/MIGRATION.md` Phase 2: Auth + Database Schema
- See `/docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` for RLS middleware pattern

title: Go API Project Scaffolding
description:
## Summary
Establish the Go API project structure, configuration management, and build/deployment pipeline. Uses Chi router with go-playground/validator.

## Scope
- Project directory structure per `/docs/ARCHITECTURE.md`
- Chi router setup with middleware chain
- Configuration management (env vars, feature flags)
- Request binding and validation helpers using go-playground/validator
- Dockerfile and container build
- Kubernetes deployment manifests
- CI/CD pipeline configuration

## Project Structure
\`\`\`
go-backend/
├── cmd/server/main.go       # Entry point
├── internal/
│   ├── config/              # Configuration loading
│   ├── server/              # HTTP server setup, Chi router
│   ├── middleware/          # Logging, recovery, request ID
│   └── handler/             # HTTP handlers (initially just health)
├── pkg/                     # Shared libraries
├── migrations/              # SQL migrations
├── Dockerfile
└── go.mod
\`\`\`

## Framework Choice: Chi
- Minimal, stdlib-compatible router
- No framework lock-in (standard http.Handler interface)
- Explicit middleware composition

## Validation Helper Pattern
\`\`\`go
var validate = validator.New()

func BindJSON[T any](r *http.Request) (T, error) {
    var req T
    if err := json.NewDecoder(r.Body).Decode(&req); err != nil {
        return req, err
    }
    return req, validate.Struct(req)
}
\`\`\`

## Deliverables
- Complete project skeleton with working health endpoint
- Docker build and local run instructions
- Kubernetes manifests (Deployment, Service, Ingress)
- CI pipeline (build, test, push to ECR)
- Makefile for common tasks

## Dependencies
- Requires: EKS Compute Platform (PLAT-b3l) - for deployment target

## References
- See `/docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` for Go API structure
- Chi router: github.com/go-chi/chi/v5
- Validator: github.com/go-playground/validator/v10

title: ElastiCache Redis Cluster
description:
## Summary
Provision ElastiCache Redis for caching, rate limiting, and Centrifugo pub/sub backend.

## Scope
Terraform modules for:
- ElastiCache Redis cluster (cluster mode disabled for simplicity)
- Subnet group (database subnets)
- Security group (allow access from EKS only)
- Parameter group with appropriate settings

## Configuration
- Node type: cache.t3.micro (staging), cache.r6g.large (prod)
- Replicas: 0 for staging, 2 for prod (Multi-AZ)
- Encryption: In-transit and at-rest
- Eviction policy: volatile-lru

## Use Cases
- Centrifugo pub/sub backend (channel presence, message routing)
- Rate limiting (sliding window counters)
- Session caching (optional, JWTs are stateless)
- API response caching (optional)

## Deliverables
- Terraform module: `infrastructure/terraform/modules/elasticache/`
- Outputs: Primary endpoint, reader endpoint, port

## Dependencies
- Requires: VPC & Networking Foundation (PLAT-03e)

## References
- See `/docs/MIGRATION.md` Phase 1: Infrastructure Foundation
- See `/docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` for caching strategy

title: SES Email Identity Setup
description:
## Summary
Configure AWS SES for Cognito email delivery. The default Cognito email limit (50/day) is too low for testing.

## Scope
- Verify domain in SES (e.g., `example.com` or `mail.example.com`)
- Add DNS records: TXT (verification), DKIM (3 CNAME records)
- Request production access (exit sandbox mode)
- Create Terraform resource for SES identity

## Terraform
```hcl
resource "aws_ses_domain_identity" "main" {
  domain = var.email_domain
}

resource "aws_ses_domain_dkim" "main" {
  domain = aws_ses_domain_identity.main.domain
}

# Output the DNS records that need to be added
output "ses_verification_token" {
  value = aws_ses_domain_identity.main.verification_token
}

output "ses_dkim_tokens" {
  value = aws_ses_domain_dkim.main.dkim_tokens
}

output "ses_identity_arn" {
  value = aws_ses_domain_identity.main.arn
}
```

## Manual Steps
1. Apply Terraform to get DNS records
2. Add records to DNS provider
3. Wait for verification (usually minutes)
4. Request production access via AWS console (Support > Service limit increase > SES sending limits)

## Deliverables
- Terraform module: `infrastructure/terraform/modules/ses/` (or inline in environment config)
- Verified domain identity
- Production access (out of sandbox)
- ARN output for Cognito module to consume

## Notes
SES is simple - this is mostly DNS verification and a support request. No complex configuration needed.
close_reason: Not needed for GCP. Identity Platform handles email internally (verification, password reset). Email templates can be customized in GCP Console. No separate email service like SES required.

comments.author: Joe Delfino
comments.text: Re-opened after AWS→GCP migration. Need to update or close based on GCP approach.

title: Go API Health & Observability
description:
## Summary
Implement health checks, structured logging, and metrics for the Go API to enable monitoring and debugging.

## Scope
- Health check endpoints (liveness, readiness)
- Structured JSON logging with request context
- Prometheus metrics endpoint
- Request tracing with correlation IDs
- Error tracking integration (optional: Sentry)

## Health Endpoints
\`\`\`
GET /healthz        # Liveness - always 200 if process is running
GET /readyz         # Readiness - checks DB, Redis, dependencies
GET /readyz?verbose # Detailed component status
\`\`\`

Readiness checks:
- Database connection pool health
- Redis connectivity
- Cognito JWKS reachable (cached)

## Structured Logging
\`\`\`go
// Using slog (Go 1.21+ stdlib)
slog.Info("request completed",
    "method", r.Method,
    "path", r.URL.Path,
    "status", status,
    "duration_ms", duration.Milliseconds(),
    "request_id", middleware.GetReqID(r.Context()),
    "user_id", user.ID,
)
\`\`\`

## Metrics
Prometheus metrics at `/metrics`:
- `http_requests_total{method, path, status}` - request counter
- `http_request_duration_seconds{method, path}` - latency histogram
- `db_pool_connections{state}` - connection pool gauge
- `db_query_duration_seconds` - query latency histogram

## Request Tracing
- Generate request ID if not present (X-Request-ID header)
- Propagate through all logs and downstream calls
- Include in error responses for debugging

## Deliverables
- \`internal/handler/health.go\` - health endpoints
- \`internal/middleware/logging.go\` - request logging
- \`internal/middleware/metrics.go\` - Prometheus instrumentation
- \`internal/middleware/requestid.go\` - correlation ID handling
- Grafana dashboard JSON (optional)

## Dependencies
- Requires: Go API Project Scaffolding (PLAT-4zm)

## References
- See `/docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` for observability requirements
- Prometheus Go client: github.com/prometheus/client_golang

title: EKS Compute Platform
description:
## Summary
Provision EKS cluster with Fargate, ECR container registry, and ALB ingress controller. This is the compute foundation for all application workloads.

## Scope
Terraform modules for:
- EKS cluster with Fargate profiles (no EC2 nodes to manage)
- ECR repositories for application images (api, executor, centrifugo)
- ECR lifecycle policies (keep last 10 images, expire untagged after 7 days)
- ALB Ingress Controller for external traffic routing
- IAM roles and OIDC provider for service accounts
- Kubernetes namespaces (app, monitoring, keda)

## Fargate Profiles
- `default` - api workloads
- `executor` - code execution pods (isolated)
- `system` - CoreDNS, ingress controller

## Deliverables
- Terraform module: `infrastructure/terraform/modules/eks/`
- Terraform module: `infrastructure/terraform/modules/ecr/`
- kubectl/helm configs for ALB ingress controller
- Outputs: Cluster endpoint, OIDC provider ARN, ECR repo URLs

## Dependencies
- Requires: VPC & Networking Foundation (PLAT-03e)

## References
- See `/docs/MIGRATION.md` Phase 1: Infrastructure Foundation
- See `/docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` for EKS topology

title: Identity Platform Authentication Infrastructure
description:
## Summary
Configure GCP Identity Platform for authentication. This replaces AWS Cognito after the GCP migration.

**Status after GCP migration:** The Terraform module at `infrastructure/terraform/modules/identity-platform/` already exists with basic configuration. This epic now covers completing the setup.

## Scope
- ✅ Identity Platform Terraform module (done in migration)
- ✅ Email/password authentication enabled
- OAuth 2.0 Web Client configuration (manual step)
- SAML provider configuration for university SSO (manual step, separate epic)
- Email templates customization (manual step)
- Validation of end-to-end auth flow

## Architecture Decision: Identity Platform for Authentication Only
Same principle as before: Identity Platform handles authentication ("who is this person?") but NOT authorization or IdP-specific profile data.

**What Identity Platform stores:** UID, email, email_verified, display name
**What it does NOT store:** University-specific claims (affiliation, eppn, department)

IdP claims pass through at login. The Go API captures these and stores them in the application database with per-tenant configurable mappings.

## Manual Configuration Required
See `terraform output manual_setup_instructions` for:
1. OAuth 2.0 Web Client creation
2. SAML provider setup (if needed)
3. Email template customization

## Deliverables
- Complete Identity Platform configuration
- OAuth client configured and credentials in tfvars
- Validated auth flow (signup, login, token refresh)
- Documentation for local development auth flow

## References
- See `/docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` for auth flow overview
- See `infrastructure/terraform/modules/identity-platform/` for module docs

title: Create Cognito Terraform module structure
description:
Set up the base Terraform module structure at infrastructure/terraform/modules/cognito/ with main.tf, variables.tf, outputs.tf, and versions.tf files.

## Follows Project Structure (PLAT-g8d)
This module follows the canonical Terraform project structure:
- Module lives in `infrastructure/terraform/modules/cognito/`
- Environment-specific values provided via `environments/{staging,prod}/terraform.tfvars`
- No hardcoded environment-specific values in module

## Common Variables
```hcl
variable "environment" {
  description = "Environment name (staging, prod)"
  type        = string
}

variable "project_name" {
  description = "Project name for resource naming"
  type        = string
}
```

## Done When
- [ ] Directory structure exists: `infrastructure/terraform/modules/cognito/`
- [ ] Files created: main.tf, variables.tf, outputs.tf, versions.tf
- [ ] Common variables defined (environment, project_name)
- [ ] `terraform validate` passes
close_reason: Module structure created during GCP migration. See infrastructure/terraform/modules/identity-platform/

title: Implement Cognito User Pool with password policies
description:
Create the aws_cognito_user_pool resource with: password policy (12+ chars, mixed case, numbers, symbols), email verification required, email-based usernames, optional MFA. Token validity: access 1hr, refresh 30 days.

## Email Configuration
Configure User Pool to use SES (depends on PLAT-7x6):
```hcl
email_configuration {
  email_sending_account = "DEVELOPER"  # Use SES, not Cognito default
  source_arn            = var.ses_identity_arn
  from_email_address    = var.email_from_address  # e.g., noreply@example.com
}
```

Required variables (no defaults):
- `ses_identity_arn` - ARN from SES module
- `email_from_address` - Verified sender address

## Custom Attributes
Do NOT define custom attributes for SAML claims (affiliation, eppn, etc). IdP-specific claims will pass through in the ID token at login and be stored in the application database per-tenant. This keeps Cognito schema-agnostic for multi-tenant flexibility.
close_reason: Identity Platform configuration implemented during GCP migration. Email/password auth enabled with password policies in module.

title: Configure OAuth 2.0 Web Client
description:
## Summary
Create and configure OAuth 2.0 Web Client for Identity Platform authentication.

## Manual Steps (GCP Console)
1. Go to: https://console.cloud.google.com/apis/credentials
2. Click "Create Credentials" > "OAuth client ID"
3. Application type: "Web application"
4. Name: "eval-{environment}-web-client"
5. Authorized JavaScript origins: Add app domains
6. Authorized redirect URIs: Add callback URLs
7. Save Client ID and Client Secret

## Update Terraform Variables
After creating OAuth client, update terraform.tfvars:
```hcl
oauth_client_id     = "your-client-id.apps.googleusercontent.com"
oauth_client_secret = "your-client-secret"
```

## Callback URLs by Environment
- Production: `https://app.example.com/auth/callback`
- Staging: `https://staging.example.com/auth/callback`
- Local dev: Uses cognito-local, not GCP

## Done When
- [ ] OAuth client created in GCP Console
- [ ] Client ID/Secret added to terraform.tfvars
- [ ] `terraform apply` succeeds with new values

title: Implement User Pool Domain
description: Create aws_cognito_user_pool_domain for hosted UI. This is required for SAML flows (covered in separate epic).
close_reason: Not needed for GCP Identity Platform. Auth domains configured automatically via authorized_domains variable.

title: Implement IAM roles for Cognito
description:
## Summary
Create IAM roles and policies required for Cognito operations.

## Scope
Minimal IAM configuration - no Identity Pool needed (Go API mediates all AWS access).

## Required Roles

### Cognito SES Sending Role (if needed)
Cognito may need a role to send emails via SES. However, this is typically handled via SES resource-based policies rather than Cognito IAM roles.

Check if SES identity policy allows Cognito:
```json
{
  "Version": "2012-10-17",
  "Statement": [{
    "Effect": "Allow",
    "Principal": {
      "Service": "cognito-idp.amazonaws.com"
    },
    "Action": "ses:SendEmail",
    "Resource": "arn:aws:ses:REGION:ACCOUNT:identity/example.com"
  }]
}
```

If resource-based policy works, no IAM role needed here.

## NOT Needed
- **Identity Pool roles** - Frontend doesn't access AWS directly; Go API mediates all AWS access via its own EKS/Fargate role
- **Unauthenticated roles** - No anonymous AWS access required

## Deliverables
- Verify SES policy allows Cognito sending (may be handled in PLAT-7x6)
- Document any IAM roles if required
- If no roles needed, document that explicitly and close task
close_reason: Not needed for GCP Identity Platform. No separate IAM roles required - Identity Platform uses project-level permissions.

title: Configure Identity Platform email templates
description:
## Summary
Customize email templates for Identity Platform verification and password reset emails.

## Manual Steps (GCP Console)
1. Go to: https://console.cloud.google.com/customer-identity/settings
2. Navigate to "Email templates" section
3. Customize:
   - Email verification template
   - Password reset template
   - Email address change template (if needed)

## Template Requirements
- Subject: Clear, branded subject lines
- Body: Include verification code/link, brief instructions
- From address: Configure custom sender if needed

## Notes
- Templates are configured in GCP Console, not Terraform
- Keep templates simple (plain text is fine initially)
- HTML templates available for better branding later

## Done When
- [ ] Email verification template customized
- [ ] Password reset template customized
- [ ] Test emails sent successfully

title: Validate Identity Platform setup
description:
## Summary
Verify Identity Platform works end-to-end after configuration.

## Validation Checklist
1. [ ] Can create test user (GCP Console or Firebase Admin SDK)
2. [ ] Verification email sent successfully
3. [ ] Can complete email verification flow
4. [ ] Can sign in with email/password
5. [ ] JWT tokens issued correctly
6. [ ] Token refresh works
7. [ ] Password reset flow works

## CLI Validation (using gcloud/Firebase Admin)
```bash
# Check Identity Platform is enabled
gcloud services list --enabled | grep identitytoolkit

# Use Firebase Admin SDK or GCP Console for user operations
```

## JWT Validation
Verify tokens contain expected claims:
- `sub` (user ID)
- `email`
- `email_verified`
- `iat`, `exp` (timestamps)

## Done When
- [ ] All checklist items verified
- [ ] Test user cleaned up
- [ ] Any issues documented

title: Local Development Environment
description:
## Summary
Set up Docker Compose-based local development environment that mirrors staging/prod architecture without requiring AWS resources.

## Architecture Decision
- **Dependencies in compose:** Postgres, Redis, cognito-local, Centrifugo
- **API runs locally:** Go API runs in devcontainer with hot reload (air)
- **Devcontainer is the only supported environment** - no macOS/Windows/Linux variations
- **Makefile is the interface:** `make dev`, `make test`, `make reset-db`

## Services
| Production | Local Equivalent |
|------------|------------------|
| RDS PostgreSQL | postgres:15 container |
| ElastiCache Redis | redis:7 container |
| Cognito | cognito-local (jagregory/cognito-local) |
| Centrifugo | centrifugo container (same image as prod) |

## Docker Compose Structure
```yaml
services:
  postgres:
    image: postgres:15
    ports: ["5432:5432"]
    volumes:
      - postgres_data:/var/lib/postgresql/data
      - ./migrations:/docker-entrypoint-initdb.d
    healthcheck: ...

  redis:
    image: redis:7
    ports: ["6379:6379"]
    healthcheck: ...

  cognito:
    image: jagregory/cognito-local
    ports: ["9229:9229"]
    volumes:
      - ./.cognito:/app/.cognito  # pre-seeded test users
    healthcheck: ...

  centrifugo:
    image: centrifugo/centrifugo:v5
    ports: ["8000:8000"]
    healthcheck: ...
```

Note: Go API is NOT in compose. It runs locally via `air` for hot reload.

## Developer Workflow
```bash
# Start everything (deps + API with hot reload)
make dev

# Just start dependencies
make deps-up

# Reset database to clean state
make reset-db

# Run tests
make test

# Check service health
make status
```

## Auth Strategy
**Decision: cognito-local** (not mock middleware)
- No dev-only code paths that could leak to prod
- Same client code everywhere - just different endpoint URL
- Tests real auth flows locally (signup, login, token refresh)
- Pre-seeded test user: test@example.com / Test1234!$
- Limitation: No SAML support (test SAML in staging)

## Deliverables
- `docker-compose.yml` in repo root (deps only)
- `.cognito/db.json` with pre-seeded test user
- `.air.toml` for hot reload configuration
- `centrifugo.json` for local Centrifugo config
- `.env.example` with local development values
- `Makefile` with all common commands
- Seed data SQL for sample namespace/class/user
- Update `.devcontainer/devcontainer.json` to install air

## Done When
- [ ] `make dev` starts all deps + API with hot reload in one command
- [ ] Changing a .go file triggers automatic rebuild (<2s)
- [ ] Pre-seeded test user can log in immediately
- [ ] Sample data exists (namespace, class) for testing
- [ ] All services have health checks
- [ ] `make reset-db` wipes and re-seeds cleanly
- [ ] Works reliably in devcontainer (the only supported environment)

## References
- See `/docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` Environments section

title: Set up Docker Compose with Postgres and Redis
description:
## Summary
Create base docker-compose.yml with Postgres and Redis for local development.

## Context
- This is a deps-only compose file (no Go API)
- API runs locally in devcontainer with hot reload
- Devcontainer is the only supported environment

## docker-compose.yml
```yaml
version: '3.8'

services:
  postgres:
    image: postgres:15
    environment:
      POSTGRES_DB: app
      POSTGRES_USER: app
      POSTGRES_PASSWORD: localdev
    ports:
      - "5432:5432"
    volumes:
      - postgres_data:/var/lib/postgresql/data
      - ./migrations:/docker-entrypoint-initdb.d:ro
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD-SHELL", "pg_isready -U app -d app"]
      interval: 5s
      timeout: 5s
      retries: 5

  redis:
    image: redis:7
    ports:
      - "6379:6379"
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD", "redis-cli", "ping"]
      interval: 5s
      timeout: 5s
      retries: 5

volumes:
  postgres_data:
```

## Notes
- Migrations mount is read-only (`:ro`) - these run on first init only
- Health checks enable proper startup ordering
- Named volume for Postgres data persistence
- cognito-local and Centrifugo added in separate subtasks

## Done When
- [ ] `docker-compose up postgres redis` starts both services
- [ ] Services have working health checks
- [ ] Can connect: `psql postgresql://app:localdev@localhost:5432/app`
- [ ] Can connect: `redis-cli -h localhost ping`
- [ ] Data persists across restarts (named volume)
close_reason: Implemented and reviewed

title: Set up local Centrifugo
description:
## Summary
Add Centrifugo to docker-compose for local WebSocket development.

## Context
- Same image as production (centrifugo/centrifugo:v5)
- Permissive config for local dev (no strict auth)
- Health check for startup ordering

## docker-compose.yml addition
```yaml
  centrifugo:
    image: centrifugo/centrifugo:v5
    command: centrifugo -c config.json
    ports:
      - "8000:8000"
    volumes:
      - ./centrifugo.json:/centrifugo/config.json:ro
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD", "wget", "-q", "--spider", "http://localhost:8000/health"]
      interval: 5s
      timeout: 5s
      retries: 5
```

## centrifugo.json (local dev config)
```json
{
  "token_hmac_secret_key": "local-dev-secret-key-not-for-production",
  "api_key": "local-api-key",
  "admin": true,
  "admin_password": "admin",
  "admin_secret": "admin-secret",
  "health": true,
  "allowed_origins": ["http://localhost:3000", "http://localhost:8080"],
  "namespaces": [
    {
      "name": "session",
      "presence": true,
      "join_leave": true,
      "history_size": 10,
      "history_ttl": "5m"
    }
  ]
}
```

## Notes
- `allowed_origins` includes typical frontend dev ports
- `admin: true` enables admin UI at http://localhost:8000
- Namespace config matches production channel structure
- API key used by Go API to publish messages

## Done When
- [ ] `docker-compose up centrifugo` starts the service
- [ ] Health endpoint responds: `curl http://localhost:8000/health`
- [ ] Admin UI accessible at http://localhost:8000 (admin/admin)
- [ ] WebSocket connection works from browser
close_reason: Implemented and reviewed

title: Configure cognito-local for local development
description:
## Summary
Configure cognito-local as the local Cognito replacement with pre-seeded test user.

## Context
- Same auth flow as prod, just different endpoint
- No dev-only code paths that could leak to prod
- Pre-seeded test user for immediate testing

## docker-compose.yml addition
```yaml
  cognito:
    image: jagregory/cognito-local
    ports:
      - "9229:9229"
    volumes:
      - ./.cognito:/app/.cognito
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD", "wget", "-q", "--spider", "http://localhost:9229"]
      interval: 5s
      timeout: 5s
      retries: 5
```

## Pre-seeded configuration
Create `.cognito/db.json` (committed to repo):
```json
{
  "Options": {
    "UserPoolDefaults": {
      "Id": "local_pool",
      "UsernameAttributes": ["email"]
    }
  },
  "UserPools": {
    "local_pool": {
      "Clients": {
        "local_client": {
          "ClientId": "local_client",
          "ClientName": "Local App",
          "UserPoolId": "local_pool"
        }
      },
      "Users": {
        "test@example.com": {
          "Username": "test@example.com",
          "Password": "Test1234!$",
          "Attributes": [
            {"Name": "email", "Value": "test@example.com"},
            {"Name": "email_verified", "Value": "true"},
            {"Name": "sub", "Value": "00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000002"}
          ],
          "Enabled": true,
          "UserStatus": "CONFIRMED"
        }
      }
    }
  }
}
```

Note: The `sub` matches the user ID in Postgres seed data for consistency.

## Go API Configuration
```go
// config/config.go
type CognitoConfig struct {
    Endpoint   string // "http://localhost:9229" for local, empty for AWS
    UserPoolID string
    ClientID   string
}

// In JWT validation, when Endpoint is set, use it instead of AWS
```

## .gitignore
```
# Ignore cognito-local runtime state, but keep the seed file
.cognito/*
!.cognito/db.json
```

## Limitations
- No SAML support (test SAML flows in staging)
- Not 100% Cognito API parity (covers common operations)
- No MFA simulation

## Done When
- [ ] cognito-local container starts with pre-seeded user
- [ ] Can sign in: test@example.com / Test1234!$
- [ ] JWT tokens are issued and can be validated
- [ ] Token refresh works
- [ ] User pool/client IDs documented in .env.example
close_reason: Implemented and reviewed

title: Create local dev documentation and scripts
description:
## Summary
Create Makefile and documentation for local development workflow.

## Context
- Devcontainer is the ONLY supported development environment
- Makefile is the primary interface for all dev tasks
- Documentation should be minimal but complete

## Makefile Targets
```makefile
.PHONY: dev deps-up deps-down wait-deps seed reset-db test status logs

# Start everything: deps + API with hot reload
dev: deps-up wait-deps
	cd go-backend && air

# Manage dependencies
deps-up:
	docker-compose up -d

deps-down:
	docker-compose down

wait-deps:
	@echo "Waiting for services..."
	@until pg_isready -h localhost -p 5432 -q; do sleep 0.5; done
	@until redis-cli -h localhost ping > /dev/null 2>&1; do sleep 0.5; done
	@until curl -sf http://localhost:9229 > /dev/null 2>&1; do sleep 0.5; done
	@echo "All services ready."

# Database management
seed:
	psql "postgresql://app:localdev@localhost:5432/app" -f scripts/seed.sql

reset-db:
	docker-compose down -v postgres
	docker-compose up -d postgres
	@until pg_isready -h localhost -p 5432 -q; do sleep 0.5; done
	$(MAKE) seed

# Testing
test:
	cd go-backend && go test ./...

# Diagnostics
status:
	@docker-compose ps

logs:
	docker-compose logs -f
```

## Documentation
Create `docs/LOCAL_DEV.md`:

```markdown
# Local Development

## Prerequisites
- Open this repo in the provided devcontainer (VS Code or GitHub Codespaces)
- That's it. The devcontainer has everything pre-installed.

## Quick Start
\`\`\`bash
make dev
\`\`\`
This starts all services and the API with hot reload. Changes to Go files auto-rebuild.

## Test Credentials
- **Email:** test@example.com
- **Password:** Test1234!$
- **Role:** instructor in "Test University" namespace

## Common Commands
| Command | Description |
|---------|-------------|
| `make dev` | Start everything with hot reload |
| `make test` | Run all tests |
| `make reset-db` | Wipe database and re-seed |
| `make logs` | Tail all service logs |
| `make status` | Show service health |

## Services
| Service | Local URL |
|---------|-----------|
| Go API | http://localhost:8080 |
| Postgres | localhost:5432 |
| Redis | localhost:6379 |
| cognito-local | http://localhost:9229 |
| Centrifugo | http://localhost:8000 |

## Troubleshooting
**Services won't start:** `make deps-down && make deps-up`
**Database issues:** `make reset-db`
**Stale containers:** `docker-compose down -v && make dev`
\`\`\`

## .env.example
```env
# Local development environment
# Copy to .env (already gitignored)

APP_ENV=local
DATABASE_URL=postgresql://app:localdev@localhost:5432/app
REDIS_URL=redis://localhost:6379
COGNITO_ENDPOINT=http://localhost:9229
COGNITO_USER_POOL_ID=local_pool
COGNITO_CLIENT_ID=local_client
CENTRIFUGO_API_URL=http://localhost:8000/api
CENTRIFUGO_API_KEY=local-api-key
```

## Done When
- [ ] Makefile with all targets works
- [ ] `docs/LOCAL_DEV.md` is complete and accurate
- [ ] `.env.example` has all required variables
- [ ] New developer can start with just `make dev`
close_reason: Implemented and reviewed

title: Set up hot reload with air
description:
## Summary
Configure air for automatic Go API rebuilds on file changes.

## Context
- API runs locally (in devcontainer), deps in docker-compose
- Devcontainer already has Go 1.22 installed
- air watches for file changes and rebuilds/restarts automatically

## Implementation

**Install air in devcontainer:**
Add to `.devcontainer/devcontainer.json` onCreateCommand:
```bash
go install github.com/air-verse/air@latest
```

**Create `.air.toml` in go-backend/:**
```toml
root = "."
tmp_dir = "tmp"

[build]
  cmd = "go build -o ./tmp/main ./cmd/server"
  bin = "./tmp/main"
  delay = 1000
  exclude_dir = ["tmp", "vendor", "node_modules"]
  exclude_regex = ["_test.go"]
  include_ext = ["go", "tpl", "tmpl", "html"]
  kill_delay = 500

[log]
  time = false

[misc]
  clean_on_exit = true
```

**Makefile integration:**
```makefile
dev: deps-up wait-deps
	cd go-backend && air

deps-up:
	docker-compose up -d

wait-deps:
	@until pg_isready -h localhost -p 5432 -q; do sleep 0.5; done
	@until redis-cli -h localhost ping > /dev/null 2>&1; do sleep 0.5; done
```

## Done When
- [ ] `make dev` starts deps + API with hot reload
- [ ] Changing a .go file triggers automatic rebuild
- [ ] API restarts within ~2 seconds of file save
- [ ] Works reliably in devcontainer
close_reason: Implemented and reviewed

title: Create seed data for local development
description:
## Summary
Create seed data so developers have working test data immediately after setup.

## Context
- Devcontainer is the only supported environment
- Need test user in cognito-local with known credentials
- Need sample data in Postgres (namespace, class, users)
- Must be idempotent (safe to run multiple times)

## Seed Data Required

**cognito-local test user:**
Pre-configure `.cognito/db.json` (committed to repo):
```json
{
  "Users": [{
    "Username": "test@example.com",
    "Password": "Test1234!$",
    "Attributes": [
      {"Name": "email", "Value": "test@example.com"},
      {"Name": "email_verified", "Value": "true"}
    ],
    "Enabled": true,
    "UserStatus": "CONFIRMED"
  }]
}
```

**Postgres seed data** (`migrations/seed.sql` or `scripts/seed.sql`):
```sql
-- Idempotent: use INSERT ... ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING

-- Test namespace
INSERT INTO namespaces (id, name, slug)
VALUES ('00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000001', 'Test University', 'test-u')
ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING;

-- Test user (matches cognito-local user)
INSERT INTO users (id, email, namespace_id, role)
VALUES ('00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000002', 'test@example.com', 
        '00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000001', 'instructor')
ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING;

-- Sample class
INSERT INTO classes (id, namespace_id, name, created_by)
VALUES ('00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000003', 
        '00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000001',
        'CS101 Introduction to Programming',
        '00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000002')
ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING;
```

**Makefile targets:**
```makefile
seed:
	psql $(DATABASE_URL) -f scripts/seed.sql

reset-db:
	docker-compose down -v
	docker-compose up -d postgres
	@until pg_isready -h localhost -p 5432 -q; do sleep 0.5; done
	# migrations run automatically via docker-entrypoint-initdb.d
	$(MAKE) seed
```

## Documentation
Add to README or LOCAL_DEV.md:
```
## Test Credentials
- Email: test@example.com
- Password: Test1234!$
- Role: instructor in "Test University" namespace
```

## Done When
- [ ] `.cognito/db.json` committed with test user
- [ ] Seed SQL creates namespace, user, sample class
- [ ] `make seed` runs idempotently
- [ ] `make reset-db` wipes and re-seeds everything
- [ ] Test credentials documented
close_reason: Implemented and reviewed

title: Add health checks and startup ordering
description:
## Summary
Ensure services start in correct order and the API waits for dependencies to be healthy.

## Context
- Postgres and Redis must be healthy before API starts
- docker-compose `depends_on` alone doesn't wait for service readiness
- Makefile handles orchestration; compose just defines services

## Implementation

**docker-compose.yml health checks:**
```yaml
services:
  postgres:
    image: postgres:15
    environment:
      POSTGRES_DB: app
      POSTGRES_USER: app
      POSTGRES_PASSWORD: localdev
    ports:
      - "5432:5432"
    volumes:
      - postgres_data:/var/lib/postgresql/data
      - ./migrations:/docker-entrypoint-initdb.d
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD-SHELL", "pg_isready -U app -d app"]
      interval: 5s
      timeout: 5s
      retries: 5

  redis:
    image: redis:7
    ports:
      - "6379:6379"
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD", "redis-cli", "ping"]
      interval: 5s
      timeout: 5s
      retries: 5

  cognito:
    image: jagregory/cognito-local
    ports:
      - "9229:9229"
    volumes:
      - ./.cognito:/app/.cognito
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD", "wget", "-q", "--spider", "http://localhost:9229"]
      interval: 5s
      timeout: 5s
      retries: 5

  centrifugo:
    image: centrifugo/centrifugo:v5
    ports:
      - "8000:8000"
    command: centrifugo -c config.json
    volumes:
      - ./centrifugo.json:/centrifugo/config.json
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD", "wget", "-q", "--spider", "http://localhost:8000/health"]
      interval: 5s
      timeout: 5s
      retries: 5
```

**Makefile wait logic:**
```makefile
wait-deps:
	@echo "Waiting for Postgres..."
	@until pg_isready -h localhost -p 5432 -q; do sleep 0.5; done
	@echo "Waiting for Redis..."
	@until redis-cli -h localhost ping > /dev/null 2>&1; do sleep 0.5; done
	@echo "Waiting for cognito-local..."
	@until curl -sf http://localhost:9229 > /dev/null 2>&1; do sleep 0.5; done
	@echo "All services ready."

dev: deps-up wait-deps
	cd go-backend && air

deps-up:
	docker-compose up -d
```

**Status check target:**
```makefile
status:
	@docker-compose ps
	@echo ""
	@echo "Service health:"
	@docker-compose ps --format json | jq -r '.[] | "\(.Name): \(.Health // "N/A")"'
```

## Done When
- [ ] All compose services have healthchecks
- [ ] `make dev` waits for all deps before starting API
- [ ] `make status` shows health of all services
- [ ] No race conditions on fresh `docker-compose up`
close_reason: Implemented and reviewed

title: RDS PostgreSQL Database
description:
## Summary
Provision RDS PostgreSQL instance for the application database. This provides the persistent data layer.

## Scope
Terraform modules for:
- RDS PostgreSQL 15+ instance
- DB subnet group (database subnets only)
- Security group (allow access from EKS only)
- Parameter group with RLS-friendly settings
- Automated backups configuration
- Optional: Read replica for prod

## Configuration
- Instance class: db.t3.micro (staging), db.r6g.large (prod)
- Storage: 20GB gp3, autoscaling to 100GB
- Multi-AZ: No for staging, Yes for prod
- Backup retention: 7 days staging, 30 days prod
- Encryption: At rest (AWS-managed keys)

## Connection Management
- Store credentials in AWS Secrets Manager
- Connection string format for Go app
- Max connections sizing for connection pooling

## Deliverables
- Terraform module: `infrastructure/terraform/modules/rds/`
- Outputs: Endpoint, port, Secrets Manager ARN

## Dependencies
- Requires: VPC & Networking Foundation (PLAT-03e)

## References
- See `/docs/MIGRATION.md` Phase 1: Infrastructure Foundation
- See `/docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` for data layer design

title: Establish Terraform project structure
description:
## Summary
Define the canonical Terraform project structure that all infrastructure modules follow. This is the foundational pattern referenced by all module structure tasks.

## Directory Structure
```
infrastructure/terraform/
├── bootstrap/                    # One-time state backend setup
│   ├── main.tf
│   └── README.md
├── modules/                      # Reusable modules (no env-specific values)
│   ├── vpc/
│   ├── eks/
│   ├── rds/
│   ├── cognito/
│   ├── ses/
│   └── ...
└── environments/                 # Environment-specific instantiation
    ├── staging/
    │   ├── main.tf               # Instantiates modules
    │   ├── backend.tf            # S3 state with staging/ prefix
    │   ├── terraform.tfvars      # Staging-specific values
    │   └── outputs.tf
    └── prod/
        ├── main.tf
        ├── backend.tf            # S3 state with prod/ prefix
        ├── terraform.tfvars      # Prod-specific values
        └── outputs.tf
```

## Environment Strategy
| Environment | Purpose | AWS Resources |
|-------------|---------|---------------|
| Local | Fast dev, unit tests | None (Docker Compose) |
| Staging | CI/CD, E2E tests, integration | Full AWS stack |
| Prod | Real users | Full AWS stack (isolated) |

## Module Design Principles
1. **Modules are environment-agnostic** - no hardcoded values
2. **Required variables have no defaults** - forces explicit config per env
3. **Environment configs provide all values** - via tfvars
4. **State isolation** - separate S3 prefixes per environment
5. **Same module, different tfvars** - staging and prod use identical module code

## Common Variables (every module should accept)
- `environment` - "staging" or "prod"
- `project_name` - for resource naming/tagging

## Configuration Flow: Modules → Apps

Infrastructure modules export outputs that applications need (connection strings, IDs, endpoints). The pattern for getting these values to running applications:

```
┌─────────────────┐     ┌─────────────────────┐     ┌─────────────┐
│ Module outputs  │ ──▶ │ Environment TF      │ ──▶ │ K8s Pod     │
│ (cognito, rds)  │     │ creates ConfigMap/  │     │ gets env    │
│                 │     │ Secret              │     │ vars        │
└─────────────────┘     └─────────────────────┘     └─────────────┘
```

### Example: Cognito → Go API

**Module exports outputs:**
```hcl
# modules/cognito/outputs.tf
output "user_pool_id" { value = aws_cognito_user_pool.main.id }
output "client_id" { value = aws_cognito_user_pool_client.main.id }
output "client_secret" { value = aws_cognito_user_pool_client.main.client_secret sensitive = true }
output "domain_url" { value = "https://${aws_cognito_user_pool_domain.main.domain}.auth.${var.region}.amazoncognito.com" }
```

**Environment TF creates K8s resources:**
```hcl
# environments/staging/main.tf
resource "kubernetes_config_map" "app_config" {
  metadata { name = "app-config" }
  data = {
    COGNITO_USER_POOL_ID = module.cognito.user_pool_id
    COGNITO_CLIENT_ID    = module.cognito.client_id
    COGNITO_DOMAIN       = module.cognito.domain_url
    AWS_REGION           = var.region
    # Add other module outputs as needed
    DATABASE_HOST        = module.rds.endpoint
    REDIS_HOST           = module.redis.endpoint
  }
}

resource "kubernetes_secret" "app_secrets" {
  metadata { name = "app-secrets" }
  data = {
    COGNITO_CLIENT_SECRET = module.cognito.client_secret
    DATABASE_PASSWORD     = module.rds.password
  }
}
```

**K8s deployment references them:**
```yaml
envFrom:
  - configMapRef:
      name: app-config
  - secretRef:
      name: app-secrets
```

### Local Development

For local dev, equivalent values go in `.env`:
```env
# .env.example (committed, no secrets)
COGNITO_ENDPOINT=http://localhost:9229
COGNITO_USER_POOL_ID=local_pool
COGNITO_CLIENT_ID=local_client
DATABASE_HOST=localhost
DATABASE_PORT=5432
REDIS_HOST=localhost
```

App code reads env vars the same way in all environments - only the source differs (K8s ConfigMap vs .env file).

## Deliverables
- [ ] Create directory structure
- [ ] Create `infrastructure/terraform/README.md` documenting the pattern
- [ ] Document ConfigMap/Secret pattern in README
- [ ] Create `.env.example` template for local dev
- [ ] Ensure PLAT-03e.8 (bootstrap) follows this structure

## References
- See `/docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` Environments section
- Pattern established for VPC in PLAT-03e.7, generalized here
close_reason: Implemented and reviewed - Terraform project structure established with bootstrap module, environment configs, module skeletons, README documentation, and .env.example

title: Go API Database Layer
description:
## Summary
Implement database connectivity with connection pooling, transactions, and RLS context middleware for the Go API.

## Scope
- PostgreSQL connection pool (pgx or database/sql with pgx driver)
- Connection configuration from environment/Secrets Manager
- Transaction management utilities
- RLS context middleware (sets session variables)
- Repository pattern base types

## Connection Pool
- Use pgxpool for connection pooling
- Configure pool size based on environment
- Health check with pool stats
- Graceful shutdown

## RLS Context Middleware
\`\`\`go
func RLSContextMiddleware(pool *pgxpool.Pool) func(http.Handler) http.Handler {
    return func(next http.Handler) http.Handler {
        return http.HandlerFunc(func(w http.ResponseWriter, r *http.Request) {
            user := auth.UserFromContext(r.Context())
            
            // Acquire connection and set session variables
            conn, _ := pool.Acquire(r.Context())
            defer conn.Release()
            
            conn.Exec(r.Context(), "SELECT set_config('app.user_id', $1, true)", user.ID)
            conn.Exec(r.Context(), "SELECT set_config('app.namespace_id', $1, true)", user.NamespaceID)
            
            // Attach conn to context for handlers
            ctx := context.WithValue(r.Context(), dbConnKey, conn)
            next.ServeHTTP(w, r.WithContext(ctx))
        })
    }
}
\`\`\`

## Transaction Utilities
\`\`\`go
func WithTx(ctx context.Context, fn func(tx pgx.Tx) error) error
\`\`\`

## Deliverables
- \`internal/db/pool.go\` - connection pool setup
- \`internal/db/tx.go\` - transaction utilities
- \`internal/middleware/rls.go\` - RLS context middleware
- \`internal/repository/base.go\` - base repository type
- Integration tests with RLS verification

## Dependencies
- Requires: Go API Project Scaffolding (PLAT-4zm)
- Requires: Database Schema & Row-Level Security (PLAT-49o)

## References
- See `/docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` for RLS middleware pattern
- pgx documentation: github.com/jackc/pgx/v5

title: Go API Auth Layer
description:
## Summary
Implement authentication middleware for JWT validation and RBAC authorization in the Go API.

## Scope
- Cognito JWT validation middleware
- JWKS (JSON Web Key Set) fetching and caching
- User context extraction from validated tokens
- Role-based access control (RBAC) middleware
- Permission checking utilities

## JWT Validation
- Fetch and cache Cognito JWKS (refresh on key rotation)
- Validate token signature, issuer, audience, expiry
- Extract claims: sub, email, cognito:groups, custom attributes

## User Context
```go
type User struct {
    ID          uuid.UUID
    Email       string
    NamespaceID uuid.UUID
    Role        Role  // admin, instructor, ta, student
    Groups      []string
}

func UserFromContext(ctx context.Context) *User
```

## RBAC Middleware
```go
// Require specific roles for route groups
r.Group(func(r chi.Router) {
    r.Use(RequireRole(RoleInstructor, RoleAdmin))
    r.Post("/classes", createClass)
})
```

## Deliverables
- `internal/auth/` package with JWT validation
- `internal/middleware/auth.go` - authentication middleware
- `internal/middleware/rbac.go` - authorization middleware
- Unit tests with mock tokens
- Integration tests against Cognito (staging environment)

## Dependencies
- Requires: Go API Project Scaffolding (PLAT-4zm)
- Requires: Cognito SAML Integration (PLAT-29h)

## References
- See `/docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` for auth middleware design
- AWS Cognito JWT structure documentation

title: E2E Auth Testing Infrastructure
description:
## Summary
Set up infrastructure for end-to-end testing of registration and authentication flows, including email verification.

## Scope
- Email receiving service for tests to read verification codes
- Test utilities for Cognito user lifecycle
- E2E tests covering: registration → email verification → login → token refresh

## Email Service Options (verify free tiers)

| Service | Free Tier | Notes |
|---------|-----------|-------|
| **Mailtrap** | 100 emails/month | Likely sufficient for E2E |
| **testmail.app** | 100 emails/month | Simple API |
| **Mailosaur** | Free trial only | No permanent free tier (~$10/mo) |
| **Ethereal** | Unlimited | Fake SMTP, good for dev, not real delivery |

**Recommendation:** Start with Mailtrap or testmail.app (free tier), switch if limits are hit.

## Test Cases
1. **Registration flow:** Sign up → receive email → extract code → verify → login
2. **Password reset:** Forgot password → receive email → extract code → reset → login
3. **Token refresh:** Login → wait → refresh token → verify still authenticated

## Implementation Notes
- Use Cognito Admin APIs for test setup/teardown (create users, clean up)
- Email service only needed for the `extract verification code` step
- Consider running full E2E sparingly (nightly) vs admin-bypass tests in CI

## Dependencies
- Requires working Cognito setup (PLAT-dar)
- Requires Go API auth endpoints (PLAT-nqj)

## Future Work
- SAML flow testing (requires mock IdP)

title: Migrate Terraform from AWS to GCP
description:
## Summary
Rewrite infrastructure Terraform from AWS to GCP to reduce monthly costs from ~$160 to ~$77 (~$1k/year savings).

## GCP Project Setup (DONE)
- **Project ID:** eval-prod-485520
- **Region:** us-east1 (South Carolina) - cheapest tier, good east coast latency
- **State bucket:** gs://eval-prod-485520-terraform-state (versioned, private)
- **Auth:** ADC with personal account for local dev
- **Budget:** $100/mo with alerts at 50%, 90%, 100%
- **APIs enabled:** compute, container, sqladmin, identitytoolkit, iam, storage

## GCP Architecture Mapping

| AWS | GCP | Notes |
|-----|-----|-------|
| EKS + Fargate | GKE Autopilot | No control plane fee |
| RDS PostgreSQL | Cloud SQL db-g1-small | ~$15/mo |
| Cognito | Identity Platform | SAML supported |
| ElastiCache | In-cluster Redis pod | $0 extra |
| ALB | Cloud Load Balancing | ~$20/mo |
| fck-nat | NAT VM (e2-micro) | ~$6/mo (not Cloud NAT @ $32) |
| S3 (state) | GCS | Already created |
| DynamoDB (locks) | GCS native locking | Built into Terraform GCS backend |
| SES | TBD (SendGrid?) | GCP has no native email |

## Current State
- **Worktree:** /workspaces/eval-terraform-foundation (feature/terraform-foundation branch)
- **PR #1:** Has AWS Terraform - needs to be replaced with GCP
- **Devcontainer:** Already updated to use gcloud CLI instead of AWS CLI

## Files to Change

### Delete (AWS-specific)
- infrastructure/terraform/modules/* - all AWS module implementations
- infrastructure/terraform/environments/* - AWS backend configs
- infrastructure/terraform/bootstrap/* - S3/DynamoDB setup

### Create (GCP)
- infrastructure/terraform/bootstrap/ - GCS backend (already exists, just reference it)
- infrastructure/terraform/modules/vpc/ - GCP VPC with subnets
- infrastructure/terraform/modules/gke/ - GKE Autopilot cluster
- infrastructure/terraform/modules/cloudsql/ - Cloud SQL PostgreSQL
- infrastructure/terraform/modules/identity-platform/ - Auth config
- infrastructure/terraform/modules/nat/ - NAT VM (e2-micro)
- infrastructure/terraform/environments/prod/ - GCP backend config

### Update
- infrastructure/terraform/README.md - GCP patterns
- .env.example - GCP-specific env vars (already has gcloud)
- docs/ARCHITECTURE.md - Update diagrams from AWS to GCP

## Cost Target
~$77/month for prod environment:
- GKE Autopilot: $0 control plane + ~$35 pods
- Cloud SQL db-g1-small: ~$15
- Cloud Load Balancing: ~$20
- NAT VM (e2-micro): ~$6
- Redis: in-cluster ($0 extra)

## Open Questions
- Identity Platform vs self-hosted Keycloak for SAML?
- SendGrid vs other email provider?
close_reason: Merged in PR #1

title: Delete AWS Terraform modules and configs
description:

title: Create GCP VPC module
description:
## Summary
Create GCP VPC module with subnets for GKE and Cloud SQL.

## Files to Create
- infrastructure/terraform/modules/vpc/main.tf
- infrastructure/terraform/modules/vpc/variables.tf
- infrastructure/terraform/modules/vpc/outputs.tf
- infrastructure/terraform/modules/vpc/versions.tf

## Requirements
- VPC in us-east1 region
- Private subnet for GKE nodes
- Private subnet for Cloud SQL (with Private Service Access)
- Public subnet for NAT VM
- Cloud Router for NAT
- Firewall rules for internal communication

## GCP Resources
- google_compute_network (VPC)
- google_compute_subnetwork (subnets)
- google_compute_router (for NAT)
- google_compute_firewall (internal rules)
- google_compute_global_address (for Private Service Access)
- google_service_networking_connection (for Cloud SQL private access)

title: Verify terraform plan succeeds
description:
## Summary
Run terraform plan to verify all GCP modules work correctly.

## Steps
1. cd to environments/prod
2. terraform init
3. terraform plan

## Expected Result
Plan should show resources to create without errors.

## Notes
This is a validation task - no actual resources will be created.
GCP credentials must be configured (ADC or service account).
close_reason: Terraform plan validation passed and reviewed
